speaker_turns.speaker_role: Director-Group Treasury & Investor Relations, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the conference call regarding ROCKWOOL International's Results for the First
Half Year of 2021. My name is Thomas Harder. I am Director of Group Treasury & Investor Relations of
ROCKWOOL International.
Today, I'm pleased to present CEO, Jens Birgersson; and CFO, Kim Junge Andersen. For the first part of this call,
all participants will be in a listen-only mode. As a reminder, this conference call is being recorded.
First, Jens Birgersson will go through our presentation and give you an update on the results for the first half year
and second quarter of 2021. Afterwards, we'll be ready to answer all your good questions.
Before I hand over the words to Jens Birgersson, I must ask you to note slide number 2, which is the forward-
looking statement. Please be aware that this presentation contains uncertainties. Now, we can go to the next
slide, which is slide number 3.
Jens Birgersson, I will now hand over the words to you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Rockwool International A/S
Thank you, Thomas. Good morning, everyone.
Today, we do this slightly differently. I'm sitting in Aarhus in Denmark. And the reason I do that is that tomorrow
we start racing in the SailGP Denmark race. I will be on the boat this afternoon myself. And this is a branding
exercise we do to become more known. And so far, this effort has worked greatly for us. We have made a couple
of videos of the previous event, and, for example, the two first videos where we mixed a bit of sustainability
message to ROCKWOOL with some information about events. We had two million views. And I think up to this
point, we never had anything that would have more than 100,000 views. So, that's a great step forward.
We also noticed with this we kind of expand the brand knowledge among future employees, and our own
employees, too, get proud from this. So, just been a very, very interesting branding investment we have done so
far with a very nice return; very motivating for us to be engaged in this.
So, I just – if you find some time, watch some of this. It will be broadcasted on TV, too. And there is also
something on YouTube called the Racing on the Edge Episode 3. It's a similar format to the one some of you
might have seen on Formula 1, the Formula 1 drivers with a similar series about this on Netflix -YouTube.
Okay. With that, I would like to move to slide 3 and we get into the numbers. We stepped into the year with a little
bit of a slow start. And then, in Q3 we saw some sort of V-plus shaped recovery. And maybe the surprising
element to what happened was that it didn't only recover to pre-pandemic level, but we saw business activity
above previous record, which were on 2019.
So, if you start with the top line half year, 11% up with almost nothing, no growth in Q1; only towards the end. On
the EBIT, €201 million year-to-date. Underlying, if I take the Rockfon settlement that happened in Q2 2019, that's
37% above 2019, and of course it's 46% above 2020 where 2020 Q2 was the worst, the most affected corona
quarter so to say. EBIT margin up, and, again, the 2020 reference is not so relevant. If I take the Rockfon
settlement up, it's more than a 10-percentage-point higher than in 2019, and that was a very good quarter.
Moving into the quarter, you see those growth numbers there, 35% growth. New high quarter for us. Good
margin. Flattish like-for-like versus 2019. 4 percentage point up versus 2020, in Q2 [ph] assignments (00:05:11)
on slide 4 now. But taking the Rockfon's US settlement out, we are more than a percentage point up on the EBIT
margin.
On the top line, what happened in that quarter was that we were quite helped by the Systems division that grew.
All the businesses grew, but especially Grodan had a bit of an exceptional quarter. We saw that competing growth
substrate like peat and cocoa, had difficulties being shipped from Asia, and that led to a substitution effect. And
we also saw at some of the harbor challenges that have been, for example, Los Angeles Harbor that the – in the
medical cannabis market that the distributors really wanted to restock, to have a bit more buffer. And that led to
an exceptional good quarter for Grodan.
And when you come to EBIT, that EBIT is maybe surprisingly good because what happened after – in Q1 we had
almost no inflation. We had energy prices that are stable. Our material input costs were stable. Logistic was on a
steady level. And then, in Q2 we saw this burst of inflation. And you may recall that we talked about the moderate
pricing strategy with the raw material pressures that we experienced there. We got a gap between price and
inflation. But due to cost reductions in the factories, some mix element, and then fixed cost productivities, the
profitability came in quite nicely.
Rockwool International A/S
Move on slide 5. Good growth. If you go down to the bottom right, the diagram there, we see that on Insulation,
and, again, I go back to 2019, so [ph] mentally (00:07:34) I give you a column of €1 billion – about €1 billion for
the half year in 2019. The Insulation business is up 5%, and I'm quite happy with that because, at this stage, we
did not – we only open up the [ph] Ran (00:07:52) factory after end of the quarter, and we had a pretty good 2019
on Insulation.
And then on the Systems division, the comparable number in 2017 was [ph] €317 million (00:08:06) turnover, and
we grew 21% above that. And there you have the Grodan effect, but also Rockfon, Rockpanel. All the businesses
really had a good quarter. And always, when we get into ramp-up stage in that regard, because we're running with
a certain number of ships in Q1, we didn't build up that much seasonal stock this year. And with the very drastic
ramp-up in demand in Q3, we had to ramp-up with bringing more ships, go higher up on the capacity utilization. It
takes a few weeks, sometimes months to do that, and we always put priority to Systems division, but they get
their product. So, very pleased with the Systems division, but also very pleased with the Insulation step-up
because it was not – no comparable.
Move onto slide 6. There, in 2Q specifically, you saw roughly 7% growth over – so the 30% versus 2020 in
Insulation is of course good. But you must remember that [indiscernible] (00:09:24) quarter it's about just short of
7% growth for the Insulation division. And the Systems, also compared to 2019, is up 30% and very good, but
really good quarter from Grodan, Rockpanel, Rockfon. But all the businesses in the Systems division have
[indiscernible] (00:09:48) double-digit growth. [ph] That's nice. (00:09:52)
Going in to the regional development on slide 7, to sum it up, I will say, in Q2 you see the normal, a little bit
slowness, not negative, but we see a bit of slowness in Sweden and Norway due to the vacation season and the
tradition in the building sector it slowed down a bit. So, there you have single-digit numbers and not so much
growth due to the June-July slowdown in building activity and vacations. But, fundamentally, all markets in
Western Europe, Eastern Europe, Russia, really solid growth. And in North America, everything double-digit,
everywhere, and with a particular boom in the US where the residential segment is reaching some sort of all-time
high I think. It's really, really growing.
Generally, across the businesses, we see the residential segment grow very well. And then as we discussed at
the previous call, office buildings and that type of activities there we don't see much growth. Although we've seen
now, for example, in France and Germany, that's starting to grow again after a long period of low activity. And
then, of course, things like logistics centers, the Amazon style [ph] tadpole (00:11:34) constructions, they are
growing very fast, also, car, electric car factories, anything logistics. Big box doing really well.
From a segment perspective, in Q2 with such a high demand for stone wool, I guess all insulation materials and
with some constraint still on some of the foam products, a lot came over to stone wool. We took a certain – and
we've seen that increasingly as our competitors in stone wool, leaving the lowest margin [indiscernible]
(00:12:13), which is the flat roof segment. And we learned 2007, 2008 that that's not a good idea. We are not
dropping that from the mix. We see it as our responsibility to make sure the flat roof suppliers get stone wool. We
don't want that market for foam, and therefore we see quite a big increase on the flat roof. And that impacted a
little bit the Insulation mix. But that's something we want to do and we want to maintain that.
We do not see long-term benefits with – that there is no stone wool for flat roof. [indiscernible] (00:13:02) after a
boom last time in 2007 and 2008 [indiscernible] (00:13:09). So, we continue with that. The mix slightly skewed
towards flat roof, [ph] and that we have evidence of this (00:13:16).
Rockwool International A/S
Profitability, if you move to slide 8, all good I would say. Going back to Q2 2019, that settlement in North America
that was in the Systems division and in the ceiling business, Rockfon business. So, that comparable, 20.7%, is
not quite fair. So, there really is an improvement in the Systems division on margins and there are couple of
factors.
So, that one factor is that they haven't seen the full inflation yet or the raw material increases because we have a
system where we slowed that down. We start to adjust their current prices. But then, generally, the mix, and the
pricing, and the cost position of the business, all of those – all of that work we have done over the last years has
paid off. So, all the businesses have improved the margin. Drawdown has a bit of especially good quarter. We
have good margin in that business, and that helps to kind of improve the Systems division margin.
On the Insulation business, good, 14.3% EBIT margin. We're happy with that, especially as the mix is a little bit
skewed towards -- a bit more flat than we normally do. Big growth in flat roof. Of course, we are up on GBI,
general building insulation...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Director-Group Treasury & Investor Relations, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Hey, Jens, just a clarification. You are commenting on slide 9 with the two, Insulation and Systems. This is slide 8
where you have EBITDA and EBIT on the [indiscernible] (00:15:08).

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. Sorry. Sorry. Okay. But let's move – I'm on slide 9, so we comment on slide 9. So, I think, generally, the
EBIT improvement on Systems division is very pleasing. And I'm happy also with the margin on Insulation
because the big raw material increase that we have seen, that most of it has impacted the Insulation business.
And in spite of that, thanks to factory improvements, productivity, fixed costs, over absorption, and some of the
personnel reductions we did last year, that then add up to this good margin.
Okay. We go to the investment activities on page 10. Here, nothing in particular has happened. We basically
progressed a little bit lower compared to last year, and that's because of different phase on the North American
factory where we have quite heavy investment last year. We did do an acquisition in Japan, a small company
north of Tokyo, a small-sized factory and quite a favorable acquisition for us. But I should also say that it's not
going to be accretive. It's going to take until early next year before we are up and producing a ROCKWOOL
product with our quality standards.
So, we're going to upgrade the factory. We have started that and we have people coming on the ground now to
start to plan that one out. But this was an opportunity that come up and we have been active in Japan for quite
some years. Importing materials of the brand is not entirely unknown, but it's nice for us to get the small factory on
the ground and start to develop that market. As you know, it's not a small economy. So, let's see how that
develops.
But don't expect impact that is quick. It takes time and day of plan, and we want to do a couple of things on that
factory before we really starts selling. It should also be said that the governmental subsidies in Japan for – in this
region are high. So, it's not going to be a capital-intensive upgrade, but you will notice in the numbers is a very,
very favorable support to upgrade in our factory. We will do that.
Rockwool International A/S
And then, we have kicked off the building – the construction on the new line in France. Obviously, no shovels in
the ground yet, but that work has started off. And then, the investment in more Rockfon capacity in Poland is
ongoing and targeted. So, we should start that line up end of first quarter 2022.
Slide 11, cash flow. Nothing much to say about that. We have a positive effect. Inventory is low. There's a positive
effect on cash, but we would have liked to have a bit more inventory if we could. But on the other hand, thanks to
a very good demand, it solved it all. So, that's one.
And then, on due to the ramp-up, quite high level of trade receivables with this slight increase here from below
10% to above 11%. That's primarily due to the trade receivables. And then, the net debt is still negative, so that's
good.
We did – slide 12. We did now just continued sustainability calls because we've had recovered a good set of
topics. And for that reason, we added in the half-year release. We won't do it in every release. But in the half-year
now and then when we do the annual release with the sustainability report, we include some slides. And then, if
something in particular comes up, we might add in another call. But half-yearly pace for this now.
So, just to give you an update on our SDG-aligned sustainability goals. So, here, we don't have the science-based
targets in. Remember, we had an old goal on CO2 per tonne produced, the CO2 efficiency goal, that we set back
in 2015/2016. In addition to this, we have the science-based target goal that is an absolute reduction goal that we
all saw progressing. And we will report more on that when we issue the sustainability report early next year.
But if we just go through the progress of these goals, we start with the CO2 emissions. We're ahead of the plan.
The biggest moves here were Norway and also the biogas in Denmark. And that's progressing, and we're going to
keep doing that. But we are ahead. That's nice.
Landfill waste. Obviously, a lot of that landfill waste, we want to put into – if it's our own material, put it in and
make product out of it. That's also ahead of plan, and we are rolling out the circularity team also so that we can
take external waste material back. The recycling team [indiscernible] (00:21:31). And so, that's ahead of plan.
That's good. Reclaimed waste linked to the landfill waste, but also growing good.
Water consumption, ahead. Water consumption but that's ahead of the goal is needs determination because
where we produce water, it's so cheap. But you spend money and you spend energy on saving something that
really doesn't cost anything. And the way water is priced at, we don't pay more for water in the summer when it's
running out to tell you to not use it for the lawn. And that's the way it's priced today with municipal water. But
maybe [ph] some of you (00:22:13) think about that. But then, here, we are doing the work and then good that
progresses because we do use water and also being more circular on water is something we do on rainwater. So,
we are investing in that and it's got great payback.
Energy efficiency of our buildings [ph] on one side, okay (00:22:33), how can you be behind on that? We are
behind because, during corona, it was difficult to get this with the boom in building activity and not having people
in our own offices. But the programs are identified. We have set the money aside for it. And we do this in maybe a
slightly different way to how you do it. I want the management team of the operational entity to be really, really
involved in the project so that we learn how to renovate our own products. So, that's fantastically fun projects but
with also a lot of work to do it because it's not just handing it over to an architect and have it done. The whole
team needs to be involved and understand how we can apply all of our product.
Rockwool International A/S
And the picture on the right is our office in Gladbeck. This building – now we haven't included the before picture,
but that building was really, really awful concrete structure. It would probably win the prize as one of the ugliest
buildings in the group. And what we have done here is that we have renovated it all with our own products. We
have used rock, panel, ventilated façade, attics. And on the top floor, we have added there you see we have
increased the number of work places. We have done Rockzero floor on top because the building was so strong
that we could add one floor by doing Rockzero walls.
And we actually aimed for German Sustainable Building Council, DGNB, silver certification. But when the building
was done, we actually got a gold and that's quite hard to get with a renovation. And we have had many politician
and municipal people visited this because with the new Fit for 55 new directives. So, the ambition there with the
higher – the increased focus on energy renovation first and to not tear buildings down, this project has gotten a lot
of attention. And the project run really smoothly. And in retrospect, it costs us a lot less than tearing this down to
make a new building. So, very proud of that.
Just to give you an example, in Denmark, I think there's only one project last year that got this gold in the whole of
Denmark and that was a new build project. So, I'm happy with that. So, nice progress. And when we look at that
energy efficiency of own footprint, so to say, our buildings, we will progress with that. But we need to complete
more projects and there's quite a lot of work. We will get better after [indiscernible] (00:25:42).
Slide 13. Really, really important slide. It's worth a full hour just to discuss this. But what you see now is that, in
the EU's ambition to be carbon-neutral or net zero, that is a net zero position by 2050 across the EU, they have
put together a program called Fit for 55 where they address energy efficiency. It's a directive for that. Emission
trading system and the CO2 forecast, energy taxation directive, and there's also a climate action on social facility
package. But a whole slate of things. And they all point towards energy efficiency first, increasing the renovation
rate of public buildings, widening the definition of scope for public buildings, raising the efficiency targets per
country per year from 0.8% to 1.5%, increasing the cost of CO2 quotas, reducing the pre-quotas. So, everything
basically speaking for more work in our area.
And I can say with the new IPCC report, all of that works need to be done. Otherwise, there's no chance to stop
the trend. And I would say, even if half of it happens, we're going to see a lot of business and there is a lot of
money into it. So, it's a lot of positive things in that.
If you look at the national recovery and resilience spending plan, EU put together €673 billion signed. €36 billion
of that is already assigned to energy efficiency renovation in 2021 to 2026.
So, there are a lot of schemes and initiatives and regulation and directives coming here. They are not decided on
yet, all of these, but they will come. And I think there is one important insight when you look at all of these
directives, that is only the energy taxation directive that need a unified decision that everyone say yes. The others
need a qualified majority vote in the EU. So, it's not enough that if two countries – we all know what countries
might go against. Some of these say no, it doesn't matter because if it's a qualified majority vote, then we'll go
ahead with it. Then, of course, that might be watered down a bit. But the sheer size of this and the direction is
good news for us. And I feel it's moving in the right direction, and this will support our business and Insulation.
And we see this big money also in North America focusing on this, in addition to the very buoyant residential
market. So, the timing of our factory in West Virginia, I mean, we are not moving as fast as we can to ramp up
production. And so far, so good. But it comes at a good time.
Rockwool International A/S
Okay. Outlook. Nothing much to say about that, really. Top line, 17%. I have put in a small beware in my outlook.
You have strong demand from many materials, so the one thing we need to be careful about is we need to get the
pallets, we need to get [ph] binder (00:29:58), we need to get stone within all those gaps. But we need to be
active on the incoming side the material into the factories. I don't see that threatening the top line. There might be
other interruption somewhere between now and Christmas maybe in the factory because of the material short
stop. That could happen, but it won't impact the number.
A little bit what I warn for is that, potentially in the construction market, if you have other construction materials
going to the site doesn't come. And we have a heavy winter, might decide to pause the project for a longer period
and then wait for the material to arrive, and then we start off in the New Year. So, I see a slight risk for that and
haven't really experienced the situation before, so I don't know how likely it is but I don't exclude it.
And then, on to the EBIT margin, we did get through although we haven't yet priced in the market so that we fully
match the inflation. We don't – we didn't do that in Q1 with mix and productivity and other improvements, we got
to a good margin. I foresee that the beginning of the second half-year, pricing will improve gradually. And then,
when we get to Q4, we're going to be in balance between inflation and price. And so, that's a translation we are
doing. We don't have the full price increase in place yet because we – due to our pricing structure for the year,
which was not doing so much in Q1. And then, the inflation came and then we have to raise our pricing ambition.
So, that's underway. But this is all in hand. It's coming with some timing issues. So, it's not well on track.
And then, on the CapEx, I would think that on the €370 million, the challenge you have when you go up on full
capacity. Five ships is a sum of the CapEx to get the outage to put it in can be difficult because we might rather
produce. So, it's a bit hard to say. But I will say, we shouldn't exceed the CapEx because the machines are
running so hard that it's actually hard to [ph] get to certainly (00:32:43) some of the maintenance CapEx.
Okay. I think that's what I have to say about the slides. With that, I would like to hand over for questions.
Rockwool International A/S

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Exane BNP Paribas
speaker_turns.text:
Good morning, everyone. Thank you for taking my question and good luck for the exciting boat race this
afternoon. Three questions, if I could. Number one is the price cost. Can you maybe help us to understand what
are your expectations regarding the cost inflation in H2 and what magnitude of price hike you've introduced in the
summer? That'd be helpful.
My second question is on the shortages that you're mentioning. Can you just be a bit more specific? Are there any
countries where you anticipate higher risk than other countries? And also, have you included the risk of those
guidance – of those shortages in your top line and margin guidance?
And my final question is looking more medium-term, you seem to be excited by the renovation wave and the
stimulus with what that can bring as a tailwind for the insulation industry. I think it's quite clear the money seems
to be here. But on the grounds, can the craftsmen actually increase the amount of jobs that they can do from
current levels or are they just running completely flat out and it all depends on the hiring potential which also looks
quite difficult given where unemployment rate is today, which is quite low already? So, what's your view on that?
Is it feasible or not? Thank you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah, yeah. So, [audio gap] (00:34:51) two and three and then Kim will do one. So, I start with the short to this
and what I put into my forecast. I have not put into the forecast that we would have the early closure of projects
because of shortages of other building materials on the site. Some of it is in. We see some of it today. But I
haven't put in in my forecast a broad based. Germany decides to shut down first of December all projects and go
home for a month. I haven't put that in, but normal ups and down. We have a little bit of risks up and down as
normal. So, it needs to be something quite big. I just want to flag it.
And those short – and then, shortages on the incoming material side, I think more or less be able to manage that
across all countries. We run it weekly every material green, red, yellow. We are very used to doing that and we
find a solution. And sometimes, if you have a particular, let's assume, you have a customer saying, I want a
certain type of pallet and we don't have the pallet, and often we can just produce another product and use – we
can work around it, working a bit with the mix and the portfolio. So, I don't see that be a big thing.
But when that then leads into your third question, I think that's a very narrow question you asked there and it's
very hard to find data on that. I think the car industry is booming. It's a bit slower to get people, blue collars in,
factory workers, but that's still a manageable amount. We've got them in.
And by the way, I should have comment that I'm missing the safety target. I missed the safety target. I should
have said that. We have this target where we improve every year what we see now that we bring people in is that,
with this flexibility we have – trips, falls, fingers, bringing new people in and getting to use to moving shifts up and
down more – we also have a little bit more challenges to not have these slips and falls. And that is reflected in the
Rockwool International A/S
safety targets. So, it might be that the three – rate of three lost time accident is a very ambitious level with this
flexibility we have. But just as a comment on that goal and that's one of the reasons we miss it.
But I think exactly what you say, evidently, the construction industry is able to install these levels of just able to
build and do what we are right now. Will the installers and builders, will we be able to create additional ones to
have the whole industry drive grow more? And what happens if it moves away with the focus on [indiscernible]
(00:38:01) embedded carbon with 75% of the carbon in the building since it's embedded in the material when you
build it. The EU would probably – as in many countries, would probably steer energy efficiency to renovating and
try to get it away from new build, and then the efficiency might be reduced even though there are not clear
numbers on that but that's what most of us believe. And then, you see dependent on construction workers.
And there, it's very hard for me just to foresee whether countries would be capable of getting more construction
workers. I'm cautious about that. I worried about that for years. And I think that's going to be a constraint we're
going to hit at some stage. And I can't say how much more we can grow – I mean, the construction market can
grow before it hits that constraint. Are we already there or is there another 10% or other ways of adding more
people to the industry. We will have to see how that goes.
Over to you, Kim, on price and cost.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Chief Financial Officer & Senior Vice President, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. Thank you very much. Even I think it's fair to say that the pricing impact in the first half has not been
anything to speak of. We also didn't mention this as one of our, you can say, drivers for profitability in the first half.
But clearly, in the second half with the price adjustments we have made in the market, we do expect and see
instead a growing impact from pricing, leading into hopefully an expected balance between the impact of – from
some sales price and cost inflation of input material in the fourth quarter. We are still talking single-digit price
increases in aggregate, so it is something that is not outrageously in the market. So, I think that's – so, my
expectation is that you will hopefully see an improved, slightly improved insulation market in this leading into at
least in Q4.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, Exane BNP Paribas
speaker_turns.text: Okay. Thank you very much to both of you.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Thank you, all. Congratulations on a good set of numbers. And I have two questions, if I may. The first one is on
the capacity constraints you talked about in the press release, having many countries at full capacity. So, could
you please talk a little more about what are those countries that are capacity constraints? And in that regard, if
you can give us what's the current utilization at the group level...

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah, yeah.
Rockwool International A/S
Q
Brijesh Siya
Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
...obviously, slightly lower in the US.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Okay. So, I don't give the utilization level. Sorry, we never do that. But I can just say one thing. We have free
capacity so we can grow next year. What I'm more worried about is labor capacity on the construction site. I would
be able to grow next year substantially, okay? But we need to start the year with building seasonal start. We can't
have a quarter where we're running on three shifts and things like that. We need to run at a high level but we have
more capacity. And we started up a machine in Norberg. We have steel ships to put on and run the whole year
and freeze less capacity. And we have Ranson line coming up. So, we can grow. But the growth worry I have for
next year would be if the labor is not there to install and keep the construction market growing.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Chief Financial Officer & Senior Vice President, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
Yeah. And, Jens, the constraint that we talk about in our report is sort of the general constraint in the construction
market because there are other materials, of course, that are – that the construction market is depending upon.
And thereby, we also become sort of constrained [indiscernible] (00:42:32) shortage of wood for instance.

speaker_turns.speaker_role: Analyst, HSBC Securities & Capital Markets (India) Pvt Ltd.
speaker_turns.text:
Understood, okay. So, when you say substantial, would it be fair to assume that, as you see right now, it's looking
like at least the 10% growth is possible next year?

speaker_turns.speaker_role: President & Chief Executive Officer, Rockwool International A/S
speaker_turns.text:
I cannot say what the market is. I'm not forecasting that we – I'm not forecasting for next year a 10% growth. We
will come with the forecast later. But what I want to say is, if the market grows 10%, I can deliver 10% growth. But

markets. So, we take that responsibility. And my view of margins is I want good margins, but I honestly don't care
too much about whether it's 13%, 14%, or 12.5%. I want to have good margin and an ability to grow the business
and have a sustainable progress in the business. I'm not interested in getting 15% margin and exploit the situation
two quarters to get some extra money there and then I'll upset every customer in the segment.
Because we can do that. My guys can do that if we want to do that, but we don't want to do that. We want to look
at it and try to tune it, so it works for our customers quarter, quarter, year, year, year, and keeps working. So, we
don't do what we did 2007, 2008 because the recall of that was awful and it took us years to fix.
So, I've said that many times. That's how we like to do. It might not swing as much up, but it doesn't swing so
much down either, and that's what we manifest here.

all participants will be in a listen-only mode. As a reminder, this conference call is being recorded.
First, Jens Birgersson will go through our presentation and give you an update on the results for the first half year
and second quarter of 2021. Afterwards, we'll be ready to answer all your good questions.
Before I hand over the words to Jens Birgersson, I must ask you to note slide number 2, which is the forward-
looking statement. Please be aware that this presentation contains uncertainties. Now, we can go to the next
slide, which is slide number 3.
Jens Birgersson, I will now hand over the words to you.

we start racing in the SailGP Denmark race. I will be on the boat this afternoon myself. And this is a branding
exercise we do to become more known. And so far, this effort has worked greatly for us. We have made a couple

message to ROCKWOOL with some information about events. We had two million views. And I think up to this
point, we never had anything that would have more than 100,000 views. So, that's a great step forward.
We also noticed with this we kind of expand the brand knowledge among future employees, and our own
employees, too, get proud from this. So, just been a very, very interesting branding investment we have done so
far with a very nice return; very motivating for us to be engaged in this.
So, I just – if you find some time, watch some of this. It will be broadcasted on TV, too. And there is also
something on YouTube called the Racing on the Edge Episode 3. It's a similar format to the one some of you
might have seen on Formula 1, the Formula 1 drivers with a similar series about this on Netflix -YouTube.
Okay. With that, I would like to move to slide 3 and we get into the numbers. We stepped into the year with a little
bit of a slow start. And then, in Q3 we saw some sort of V-plus shaped recovery. And maybe the surprising
element to what happened was that it didn't only recover to pre-pandemic level, but we saw business activity
above previous record, which were on 2019.
So, if you start with the top line half year, 11% up with almost nothing, no growth in Q1; only towards the end. On
the EBIT, €201 million year-to-date. Underlying, if I take the Rockfon settlement that happened in Q2 2019, that's
37% above 2019, and of course it's 46% above 2020 where 2020 Q2 was the worst, the most affected corona
quarter so to say. EBIT margin up, and, again, the 2020 reference is not so relevant. If I take the Rockfon
settlement up, it's more than a 10-percentage-point higher than in 2019, and that was a very good quarter.
Moving into the quarter, you see those growth numbers there, 35% growth. New high quarter for us. Good
margin. Flattish like-for-like versus 2019. 4 percentage point up versus 2020, in Q2 [ph] assignments (00:05:11)
on slide 4 now. But taking the Rockfon's US settlement out, we are more than a percentage point up on the EBIT
margin.
On the top line, what happened in that quarter was that we were quite helped by the Systems division that grew.
All the businesses grew, but especially Grodan had a bit of an exceptional quarter. We saw that competing growth
substrate like peat and cocoa, had difficulties being shipped from Asia, and that led to a substitution effect. And
we also saw at some of the harbor challenges that have been, for example, Los Angeles Harbor that the – in the
medical cannabis market that the distributors really wanted to restock, to have a bit more buffer. And that led to
an exceptional good quarter for Grodan.
And when you come to EBIT, that EBIT is maybe surprisingly good because what happened after – in Q1 we had
almost no inflation. We had energy prices that are stable. Our material input costs were stable. Logistic was on a
steady level. And then, in Q2 we saw this burst of inflation. And you may recall that we talked about the moderate
pricing strategy with the raw material pressures that we experienced there. We got a gap between price and
inflation. But due to cost reductions in the factories, some mix element, and then fixed cost productivities, the
profitability came in quite nicely.

From a segment perspective, in Q2 with such a high demand for stone wool, I guess all insulation materials and
with some constraint still on some of the foam products, a lot came over to stone wool. We took a certain – and
we've seen that increasingly as our competitors in stone wool, leaving the lowest margin [indiscernible]
(00:12:13), which is the flat roof segment. And we learned 2007, 2008 that that's not a good idea. We are not
dropping that from the mix. We see it as our responsibility to make sure the flat roof suppliers get stone wool. We
don't want that market for foam, and therefore we see quite a big increase on the flat roof. And that impacted a

We do not see long-term benefits with – that there is no stone wool for flat roof. [indiscernible] (00:13:02) after a

The biggest moves here were Norway and also the biogas in Denmark. And that's progressing, and we're going to

Landfill waste. Obviously, a lot of that landfill waste, we want to put into – if it's our own material, put it in and

really doesn't cost anything. And the way water is priced at, we don't pay more for water in the summer when it's

maybe [ph] some of you (00:22:13) think about that. But then, here, we are doing the work and then good that
progresses because we do use water and also being more circular on water is something we do on rainwater. So,
we are investing in that and it's got great payback.
Energy efficiency of our buildings [ph] on one side, okay (00:22:33), how can you be behind on that? We are
behind because, during corona, it was difficult to get this with the boom in building activity and not having people
in our own offices. But the programs are identified. We have set the money aside for it. And we do this in maybe a
slightly different way to how you do it. I want the management team of the operational entity to be really, really
involved in the project so that we learn how to renovate our own products. So, that's fantastically fun projects but
with also a lot of work to do it because it's not just handing it over to an architect and have it done. The whole
team needs to be involved and understand how we can apply all of our product.

And the picture on the right is our office in Gladbeck. This building – now we haven't included the before picture,
but that building was really, really awful concrete structure. It would probably win the prize as one of the ugliest
buildings in the group. And what we have done here is that we have renovated it all with our own products. We
have used rock, panel, ventilated façade, attics. And on the top floor, we have added there you see we have
increased the number of work places. We have done Rockzero floor on top because the building was so strong
that we could add one floor by doing Rockzero walls.
And we actually aimed for German Sustainable Building Council, DGNB, silver certification. But when the building
was done, we actually got a gold and that's quite hard to get with a renovation. And we have had many politician
and municipal people visited this because with the new Fit for 55 new directives. So, the ambition there with the
higher – the increased focus on energy renovation first and to not tear buildings down, this project has gotten a lot
of attention. And the project run really smoothly. And in retrospect, it costs us a lot less than tearing this down to
make a new building. So, very proud of that.
Just to give you an example, in Denmark, I think there's only one project last year that got this gold in the whole of
Denmark and that was a new build project. So, I'm happy with that. So, nice progress. And when we look at that
energy efficiency of own footprint, so to say, our buildings, we will progress with that. But we need to complete
more projects and there's quite a lot of work. We will get better after [indiscernible] (00:25:42).
Slide 13. Really, really important slide. It's worth a full hour just to discuss this. But what you see now is that, in
the EU's ambition to be carbon-neutral or net zero, that is a net zero position by 2050 across the EU, they have
put together a program called Fit for 55 where they address energy efficiency. It's a directive for that. Emission
trading system and the CO2 forecast, energy taxation directive, and there's also a climate action on social facility
package. But a whole slate of things. And they all point towards energy efficiency first, increasing the renovation
rate of public buildings, widening the definition of scope for public buildings, raising the efficiency targets per
country per year from 0.8% to 1.5%, increasing the cost of CO2 quotas, reducing the pre-quotas. So, everything
basically speaking for more work in our area.
And I can say with the new IPCC report, all of that works need to be done. Otherwise, there's no chance to stop
the trend. And I would say, even if half of it happens, we're going to see a lot of business and there is a lot of
money into it. So, it's a lot of positive things in that.
If you look at the national recovery and resilience spending plan, EU put together €673 billion signed. €36 billion
of that is already assigned to energy efficiency renovation in 2021 to 2026.
So, there are a lot of schemes and initiatives and regulation and directives coming here. They are not decided on
yet, all of these, but they will come. And I think there is one important insight when you look at all of these
directives, that is only the energy taxation directive that need a unified decision that everyone say yes. The others
need a qualified majority vote in the EU. So, it's not enough that if two countries – we all know what countries
might go against. Some of these say no, it doesn't matter because if it's a qualified majority vote, then we'll go
ahead with it. Then, of course, that might be watered down a bit. But the sheer size of this and the direction is
good news for us. And I feel it's moving in the right direction, and this will support our business and Insulation.
And we see this big money also in North America focusing on this, in addition to the very buoyant residential
market. So, the timing of our factory in West Virginia, I mean, we are not moving as fast as we can to ramp up
production. And so far, so good. But it comes at a good time.

Okay. Outlook. Nothing much to say about that, really. Top line, 17%. I have put in a small beware in my outlook.
You have strong demand from many materials, so the one thing we need to be careful about is we need to get the
pallets, we need to get [ph] binder (00:29:58), we need to get stone within all those gaps. But we need to be
active on the incoming side the material into the factories. I don't see that threatening the top line. There might be

stop. That could happen, but it won't impact the number.

and then wait for the material to arrive, and then we start off in the New Year. So, I see a slight risk for that and

match the inflation. We don't – we didn't do that in Q1 with mix and productivity and other improvements, we got
to a good margin. I foresee that the beginning of the second half-year, pricing will improve gradually. And then,
when we get to Q4, we're going to be in balance between inflation and price. And so, that's a translation we are
doing. We don't have the full price increase in place yet because we – due to our pricing structure for the year,
which was not doing so much in Q1. And then, the inflation came and then we have to raise our pricing ambition.

And then, on the CapEx, I would think that on the €370 million, the challenge you have when you go up on full
capacity. Five ships is a sum of the CapEx to get the outage to put it in can be difficult because we might rather
produce. So, it's a bit hard to say. But I will say, we shouldn't exceed the CapEx because the machines are
running so hard that it's actually hard to [ph] get to certainly (00:32:43) some of the maintenance CapEx.
